nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coral reef flats, lagoons, and seaward reefs; depth range 1–50 m, most commonly 1–20 m

diet.notes: Primarily grazes on macroalgae and microalgae including filamentous algae, nori, spirulina, and seaweed sheets. Has been documented switching from fine-textured filamentous algae to coarser algae as it grows. Accepts dried seaweed clips and herbivore-formulated pellets. A well-fed specimen is advised to prevent coral nipping.

summary: A peaceful, venomous-spined herbivore from Indo-Pacific reef flats, generally advised for established reef systems with ample algae. Known for effective hair-algae control and a mild temperament toward most tankmates.
careNotes: The Little Spinefoot is generally considered one of the easier rabbitfish to keep, though moderate care is advised due to its size requirements and dietary needs. A tank of roughly 440 L (around 115 gallons) or larger is generally recommended, with ample open swimming space and live rock for grazing. Venomous dorsal and anal fin spines can inflict a painful sting — handling with a net is strongly advised and bare-hand contact should be avoided. Like other Siganidae, this species tends to be aggressive toward conspecifics and other rabbitfish; keeping only one per tank is generally recommended unless in a very large system. The species has been reported to adopt a mottled dark camouflage pattern when stressed or sleeping — this is normal and not a sign of illness. Reef safety is conditional: well-fed specimens have been reported to leave corals alone, but underfed fish may nip at LPS and soft corals. Schools of fewer than ten individuals have been documented in the wild; captive specimens are typically kept singly. Water parameter cross-source note: temperature ranges varied across sources (22–26°C per trade sources; 24–28°C per FishBase); the averaged range of 23–27°C is used here. Maximum adult size similarly varied (20–29 cm across sources); the averaged maximum of approximately 26 cm is used.
